lessons.title: std::generator: Lazy Sequences with Coroutines

background.label: C++20: coroutines
background.body: std::generator builds on the C++20 coroutine mechanism: functions with co_yield, co_return, co_await. A coroutine is a function that can be suspended and resumed. std::generator encapsulates the canonical 'generator' pattern on top of this mechanism.

lessons.motivation: Before C++23, writing a lazy generator with coroutines meant implementing your own `promise_type` with `yield_value`, `return_void`, `initial_suspend`, `final_suspend`, and iterator adapters — 50+ lines of boilerplate for trivial cases. Libraries like cppcoro and range-v3 filled the gap, but nothing was standard. C++23 introduces `std::generator<T>`: a standard adapter that reduces a lazy sequence to a function with `co_yield`, with the result immediately compatible with all range algorithms and views.
lessons.theory:
Before C++23, creating a lazy generator required writing your own coroutine promise type: a nested `promise_type` with `yield_value`, `return_void`, `initial_suspend`, `final_suspend`, and iterator adapters to make values iterable in a `for` loop. For trivial cases this meant 50+ lines of boilerplate with no business logic whatsoever.

C++23 standardizes the `std::generator<T>` template in the `<generator>` header. A generator function is an ordinary function returning `std::generator<T>` that contains `co_yield`. When `co_yield value` is executed, the coroutine hands the value to the consumer and suspends; it resumes when the consumer requests the next element. Generators are lazy: computation happens only on demand. A `std::generator<T>` object is move-only — the coroutine frame cannot be copied, only moved — and can be iterated exactly once.

The return type is parameterized on the value type: `std::generator<int>` yields `int` values, `std::generator<int&>` yields references (useful for expensive objects to avoid copies), and `std::generator<const int&>` yields const references. For nested generators, C++23 introduces `std::ranges::elements_of(range)`: passing it to `co_yield` flattens an entire range or nested generator into the current generator's value stream without intermediate buffering.

`std::generator<T>` satisfies `std::ranges::input_range`, so it composes naturally with view pipelines: `gen | std::views::take(10)`, `gen | std::views::filter(pred) | std::views::transform(fn)`, and so on. This enables a clean pattern: the generator describes a potentially infinite or expensive-to-compute sequence, while views control how many elements are actually materialized.

**What a coroutine is.** A coroutine is a function that can be suspended mid-execution and resumed later from exactly the same point. When it hits `co_yield`, it produces a value and pauses — its stack and all local variables are preserved. The caller receives the value, does something with it, and on the next iteration resumes the coroutine, which continues from where it left off as if nothing had interrupted it. This is fundamentally different from an ordinary function, which starts from scratch on every call.

**The three coroutine keywords.** A function becomes a coroutine if it uses any of: `co_yield expr` — produce a value and suspend; `co_return expr` — terminate the coroutine and optionally deliver a final value; `co_await awaitable` — suspend until an asynchronous operation completes (not used in generators, but part of the general mechanism). A generator function typically uses only `co_yield` and an implicit `co_return` at the end of its body.

**The coroutine frame.** When you call a generator function, it does not start executing immediately — instead, a *frame* is allocated on the heap to store all local variables and the current execution point. The caller receives a handle object (here, `std::generator<T>`). Actual execution begins only when the consumer requests the first element (the first iteration step). This is why generators are lazy: no computation happens until someone iterates.

**promise_type — the protocol behind the magic.** Internally, every coroutine type defines a nested `promise_type` that governs its behavior: what happens on `co_yield`, whether to suspend immediately at the call site, what to do on completion. `std::generator<T>` ships a ready-made `promise_type` — that is the entire point of the type. The generator's iterator implements `operator++`, which resumes the coroutine to the next `co_yield`, and `operator*`, which reads the yielded value. A `for (auto v : gen)` loop calls these alternately — the coroutine and its caller trade control back and forth through suspension points.

examples.title: Infinite Fibonacci Sequence
examples.description: A generator for the infinite Fibonacci sequence. `co_yield a` hands the current value to the caller and suspends; `std::views::take(10)` materializes only the first ten elements.

examples.title: View Pipelines and Nested Generators via elements_of
examples.description: Two uses of `std::generator` with ranges: (1) a `range` generator is combined with `std::views::transform` to produce squares; (2) a `concat` generator uses `co_yield std::ranges::elements_of(...)` to splice a nested generator's elements into its own stream without intermediate buffering.

examples.title: Execution Order: Coroutine Under the Microscope
examples.description: The generator prints diagnostic lines at each point in its execution; `main` does the same. The order of lines in the output reveals the real interleaving: the coroutine and the caller do not run in parallel — they take turns, transferring control through each `co_yield`. Notice that creating the generator (`auto gen = execution_trace()`) does not start the coroutine body — that only happens at the first iteration step.
examples.expectedOutput:
[main] creating generator
[main] starting iteration
[coroutine] started
[main] got: first value
[coroutine] resumed after first yield
[main] got: second value
[coroutine] resumed after second yield
[main] got: third value
[coroutine] reached end — returning
[main] iteration complete
